metadata.title: Why a 99%-Accurate Test Is Almost Always Wrong When It Says Yes
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Computational Skepticism candidate 01. Source chapter: books/computational-skepticism-for-ai/chapters/02-probability-uncertainty-and-the-confidence-illusion.md
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, dusty blue = the one true positive / the truth, terracotta = false alarms / the noise, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: dusty blue #5B7B9C = the true positive / truth; terracotta #D35F43 = false alarms / noise; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: All numbers verified by direct computation (FACTCHECK.md): base rate 1/10,000; 99% sensitivity AND specificity (the chapter's stated simplification); 1 true positive vs 9,999 x 0.01 = ~100 false positives; posterior = 1/101 = ~1%. Bayes cross-check: 0.0001x0.99 / (0.0001x0.99 + 0.9999x0.01) = 0.0098. Card exclusions: no Bayes formula on screen, no sensitivity/specificity vocabulary, no odds-form shortcut, no second domain example. The 10,000-dot grid must be countable-true: exactly 10,000 marks, exactly 1 blue, exactly 100 terracotta.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A test that is ninety-nine percent accurate just came back positive. And the patient is almost certainly fine. Both halves of that sentence are true.

card.copy: Why a 99% accurate test is almost always wrong when it says yes
card.sub: base rates and the confidence illusion

beats.narration_text: The disease is rare — one person in ten thousand has it. The test comes back positive. So: how confident are you that this patient is sick?
beats.scene_description: a printed lab-result slip pinned to the newsprint ground, POSITIVE stamped on it, clinical and plain — slow push-in on the word
beats.image_prompt: printed medical lab test result slip with a bold POSITIVE result line, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, desaturated flat print reproduction, editorial collage, no brand names

beats.narration_text: Lock in a number before we count. Most engineers say ninety percent or higher — the reasoning feels airtight: accurate test, positive result, sick patient.

document.quote: Pick a number. Lock it in.

beats.scene_description: the dare set large in charcoal serif; gold highlighter sweeps 'Lock' as it is spoken

beats.narration_text: So let's take ten thousand people and simply count. In this entire crowd, on average, exactly one person actually has the disease.

production_viz.label: 10,000 people — 1 is sick
production_viz.mechanic: a 125x80 grid of 10,000 pale dots fills the frame in row-chunks; one dot turns dusty blue and pulses once

beats.narration_text: The test is ninety-nine percent accurate, so it catches that one person. One true positive.
beats.scene_description: the grid persists; a small blue flag chip 'TRUE POSITIVE' plants beside the blue dot as the test sweeps past it

production_viz.mechanic: a thin scan line sweeps the grid once; the blue dot gets a blue flag chip
production_viz.note: no ring here — the ring is saved for B09 (one editor's-pen voice per graphic)

beats.narration_text: But that same ninety-nine percent means the test is wrong about one percent of the nine thousand nine hundred ninety-nine healthy people. That's about a hundred false alarms.

production_viz.label: ~100 false positives
production_viz.mechanic: exactly 100 scattered dots flip terracotta in one accumulating wave across the grid

beats.narration_text: Now pull out everyone who tested positive. One hundred and one people. One of them is sick. A hundred of them are fine.

production_viz.label: the positive pool
production_viz.mechanic: the grid fades back; the 101 flagged dots migrate into a bucket outline on the right and settle into tidy rows — 100 terracotta, 1 blue on top

beats.narration_text: So given a positive result, the chance this patient is actually sick is one in a hundred and one. About one percent.
beats.scene_description: the answer set enormous — '≈1%' in charcoal serif — with the counting line beneath as attribution; no formula anywhere (card exclusion)

beats.narration_text: And here's the part that should bother you: nothing is broken. The test did exactly what the box promised. Your intuition was off by two orders of magnitude.
beats.scene_description: the 99% box-claim and the ≈1% answer sit side by side; the editor's pen draws one terracotta ring around the gap between them — around the 'two orders of magnitude'

production_viz.mechanic: two chips — '99% accurate' (the box) and '≈1% sick' (the count) — with a HandRing landing on the space between, timed to 'orders of magnitude'
production_viz.note: one ring only; this is the film's single ring

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] the ring on the two-orders gap

beats.narration_text: Because you answered the wrong question. How often is the test right about sick people? Ninety-nine percent. How often is a positive actually sick? One percent.

production_viz.label: two different questions
production_viz.mechanic: two question cards side by side — 'right about the sick?' answering 99 in blue, 'positive actually sick?' answering ~1 in terracotta — the numbers land as each is spoken
production_viz.note: the direction of the conditional, without the word 'conditional'

beats.narration_text: The bridge between them is the ingredient intuition always forgets: how rare the disease was before the test ever ran. The base rate.

production_viz.label: the forgotten ingredient
production_viz.mechanic: the 10,000 grid ghosts back at low opacity behind a single chip: '1 in 10,000 — the base rate'; the one blue dot still burns through

beats.new_visual_element: [CALLBACK] the crowd returns behind the base-rate chip

beats.narration_text: An honest test and a rare disease add up to a pile of false alarms. So before you trust any flag — count the crowd it was raised in.

card.copy: The test is honest. Your intuition forgot the prior.
card.sub: from Computational Skepticism for AI — chapter 2
